nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Goldesborough
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from either of two places in North Yorkshire called Goldsborough. One, near Knaresborough, is named from the Old English personal name Godel + Old English burh ‘fortified place’. The other, near Whitby, is named from the Old English personal name Golda + burh.

variants.name: Goolsby
meanings: English: probably a shortened form of Gooldsbury, a variant of Goldsborough .

variants.name: Gulsby
meanings: Probably a shortened form of English Gooldsbury, a variant of Goldsborough .

variants.name: Goldsby
meanings: English: apparently a habitational name from a lost or unidentified place, probably in southern or eastern England unless it is a variant of   Goldsborough   due to early migration.

nameMeanings.name: Isolda
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: The name of the tragic mistress of Tristan in the Arthurian romances. There are several versions of the story. The main features are that the beautiful Isolde, an Irish princess, is betrothed to the aged King Mark of Cornwall. However, through accidentally drinking a magic potion, she and the young Cornish knight Tristan fall in love, with tragic consequences. The story has exercised a powerful hold on the European imagination. The name was relatively common in Britain in the Middle Ages, but is much rarer today.

en.lifeSummary: Isolda de Goldesborough was born about 1230, in Manor, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. She married John de Bolton I about 1255, in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son. She died about 1286, in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 58.
